_provenance: Claude-authored original Gaokao reading, batch D5 (completes package D to 50). ~220-280 word passages, 4 MCQs each. Gaokao difficulty. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage: Sarah had always wanted to be a nurse, but her path was far from easy. She came from a poor family, failed her first nursing exam, and was told by more than one person that the job was too demanding for someone like her. Yet she refused to abandon her dream. Working in a shop by day, she studied late into the night, and on her second attempt, she passed. Sarah chose to work not in a comfortable city hospital, but in a small clinic in a remote rural area, where doctors were few and the need was great. There, she was often the only medical help for miles. She delivered babies, treated injuries, and travelled long distances on foot to reach patients who could not come to her. The work was exhausting and the pay was low, but Sarah found a deep meaning in it that no city job could have given her. To the people of the region, she was far more than a nurse; she was a trusted friend, often the first person they turned to in times of trouble. After thirty years, Sarah finally retired. The whole community came to thank her, and many wept. She had no fortune to show for her life's work, no fame beyond her valley. But she had something rarer: the knowledge that she had spent her life doing work that truly mattered, in a place where she was truly needed. 'I was never rich,' she said, 'but I was never poor either.'

questions.prompt: What obstacles did Sarah face?
options: A poor family, a failed first exam, and discouragement.

questions.prompt: Where did Sarah choose to work?
options: A small clinic in a remote rural area.

questions.prompt: What was Sarah to the local people?
options: Far more than a nurse — a trusted friend.

questions.prompt: What did Sarah mean by 'never rich but never poor'?
options: She secretly had a lot of money.
Though she had no fortune, her work had deep meaning and worth.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 出身贫寒、初考落榜、被劝退)、细节(B 偏远乡村小诊所)、细节(B 不只是护士、更是被信赖的朋友)、主旨/态度(B 虽无财富、工作却极有意义与价值)。

sets.passage: In the South American city of Medellín, some of the poorest neighbourhoods cling to steep hillsides high above the centre. For years, the people living there were cut off from the rest of the city. The journey down to where the jobs were could take hours on foot or by crowded bus along winding roads. This isolation trapped many in poverty. Then the city tried something unusual. Instead of building more roads, it built a cable car — the kind usually seen carrying tourists up mountains — as a form of public transport. The cars glide silently over the rooftops, connecting the hillside communities directly to the city's main transport network below. The effect was remarkable. A journey that once took hours now takes minutes. Suddenly, people living high on the hills could reach jobs, schools and hospitals in the city with ease. But the benefits went far beyond transport. The cable car brought a sense of pride and connection to neighbourhoods that had long felt forgotten. Around its stations, the city built libraries, parks and schools, and crime in the area fell sharply. Medellín's cable car has since inspired similar projects in other cities. Its success carries a wider lesson: that the solutions to a city's problems are not always the obvious ones, and that connecting people — physically and socially — can transform lives in ways that go far beyond simply getting from one place to another.

questions.prompt: What problem did the hillside neighbourhoods face?
options: They were cut off from the city, which trapped people in poverty.

questions.prompt: What unusual solution did the city build?
options: A cable car as a form of public transport.

questions.prompt: What benefits went beyond transport?
options: Pride, connection, lower crime, and new libraries and parks.

questions.prompt: What wider lesson does the project carry?
options: That cable cars are only for tourists.
That solutions are not always obvious, and connecting people can transform lives.
That building roads is always best.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 与城市隔绝、困于贫穷)、细节(B 用缆车作公共交通)、细节(B 带来自豪、连接、犯罪下降、新设施)、主旨/推断(B 解决之道未必显而易见、连接人能改变生活)。

sets.passage: Twice a year, billions of birds undertake one of the most astonishing journeys in the natural world. As the seasons change, they migrate, some flying thousands of miles between their summer and winter homes. The Arctic tern, for example, travels from the top of the world to the bottom and back each year, a round trip of tens of thousands of miles. How birds find their way across such vast distances has long puzzled scientists. They appear to use a remarkable combination of tools. By day, they navigate by the position of the sun; by night, by the stars. Most amazingly, many birds can sense the Earth's magnetic field, giving them a kind of in-built compass that works even when the sky is hidden by cloud. These journeys are full of danger. Birds must cross oceans, deserts and mountains, facing storms, exhaustion and hunger along the way. Many do not survive. To prepare, they eat enormously before setting out, building up the fat reserves that will fuel their flight. Today, these ancient journeys face a new threat: human activity. As we destroy the wetlands and forests where migrating birds rest and feed, and as the climate changes the timing of the seasons, many species are struggling. Protecting migratory birds is difficult, because it requires countries all along their routes to work together. Their journeys remind us of a simple truth: that the natural world knows no borders, and that to protect it, neither can we.

questions.prompt: What does the Arctic tern do each year?
options: It travels from the top of the world to the bottom and back.

questions.prompt: How do migrating birds navigate?
options: By the sun, the stars, and the Earth's magnetic field.

questions.prompt: How do birds prepare for migration?
options: They eat enormously to build up fat reserves.

questions.prompt: What truth do their journeys remind us of?
options: That the natural world knows no borders, so protecting it requires cooperation.
That migration is easy and safe.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 从世界之巅飞到尽头再返回)、细节(B 借太阳/星辰/地磁导航)、细节(B 出发前大量进食储脂)、主旨/推断(B 自然无国界、保护需各国合作)。

sets.passage: The clothes we wear come at a hidden cost. The fashion industry, especially the fast, cheap clothing that fills our shops, is one of the most polluting industries in the world. Producing it uses enormous amounts of water and energy, and because the clothes are cheap and quickly go out of style, huge quantities are thrown away after being worn only a few times. In response, a movement towards 'sustainable fashion' is growing. Its ideas are simple but powerful. The first is to buy less and choose well: fewer, better-made clothes that last for years instead of many cheap ones that quickly fall apart. The second is to give clothes a longer life — repairing them when they tear, and buying and selling second-hand rather than always buying new. Second-hand clothing, once seen by some as a sign of poverty, has become increasingly popular and even fashionable, especially among young people. Buying used clothes saves money, reduces waste, and can even be a way of finding unique items with character that no shop could sell. None of this means we must stop enjoying clothes or expressing ourselves through what we wear. The point is to do so more thoughtfully. Every garment we keep instead of throwing away, every used item we choose instead of a new one, makes a small difference. In fashion, as in much of life, the most sustainable choice is often simply to value and care for what we already have.

questions.prompt: Why is fast fashion described as a problem?
options: It is highly polluting and much of it is thrown away after little use.

questions.prompt: What is one idea of sustainable fashion?
options: To buy as much as possible.
To buy fewer, better clothes and give them a longer life.
To throw clothes away even faster.
To stop wearing clothes at all.

questions.prompt: How is second-hand clothing now seen?
options: Only as a sign of poverty.
As increasingly popular and even fashionable.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say is the most sustainable choice?
options: To value and care for what we already have.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 高污染、穿几次就丢)、细节(B 少买精选、延长衣物寿命)、细节(B 二手衣日益流行甚至时髦)、主旨/推断(B 最可持续之选常是珍惜并照护已有之物)。

sets.passage: For years, the patch of land at the end of Elm Street was an ugly, forgotten place, filled with weeds and rubbish. People hurried past it, and no one imagined it could be anything else. No one, that is, except an old man named Mr. Foster. A retired gardener living nearby, Mr. Foster could not bear to see the land go to waste. With no permission and no money, he simply began, one morning, to clear away the rubbish himself. Day after day, while neighbours watched with curiosity, he dug, planted, and watered. Many thought he was wasting his time. But slowly, the dead land came back to life. Flowers bloomed where rubbish had lain; a few vegetables appeared; a small tree took root. As the garden grew more beautiful, something changed in the neighbourhood too. Curious neighbours began to stop and talk, then to help. Children came to plant seeds; an old woman donated flowers; a young man built a bench. What had begun as one man's quiet labour became a shared project, and the once-ugly patch of land became the pride of the street, a place where neighbours met and children played. When asked why he had started it all, Mr. Foster simply smiled. 'I didn't really plant a garden,' he said. 'I planted an idea, and let it grow.' His story is a gentle reminder that change often begins with a single person willing to act, while everyone else is still waiting.

questions.prompt: What was the land at the end of Elm Street like at first?
options: An ugly place full of weeds and rubbish.

questions.prompt: What did Mr. Foster do?
options: Began clearing and planting it himself, without permission or money.

questions.prompt: What happened as the garden grew?
options: Neighbours began to help, and it became the pride of the street.

questions.prompt: What does Mr. Foster's story remind us?
options: That change often begins with a single person willing to act.
That old men should simply rest.
That empty land is always useless.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 杂草垃圾的丑地)、细节(B 无许可无钱、自己动手清理种植)、细节(B 邻居加入、成了街区骄傲)、主旨(B 改变常始于一个愿意行动的人)。

sets.passage: Many of us do it every night: we lie in bed, phone in hand, scrolling through messages, videos and news until our eyes grow heavy. It feels relaxing, a harmless way to wind down before sleep. But scientists warn that this common habit may be quietly harming our rest. The problem is partly the light. Screens give off a blue light that is similar to daylight. When our eyes take in this light at night, it tricks the brain into thinking it is still daytime, reducing the production of the natural chemical that makes us feel sleepy. As a result, we take longer to fall asleep and sleep less deeply. But the content is a problem too. Exciting videos, worrying news, or messages that demand a reply keep our minds active and alert, exactly when we need them to be winding down. A device designed to capture our attention is the last thing a tired brain needs. The solution scientists suggest is simple, if not always easy: keep phones out of the bedroom, or at least put them away an hour before sleep. Reading a paper book, listening to quiet music, or simply sitting in dim light all help the body prepare for rest. In a world that never switches off, learning to disconnect at night may be one of the simplest and most valuable things we can do for our health.

questions.prompt: What habit does the passage discuss?
options: Using phones in bed before sleep.

questions.prompt: Why is screen light a problem at night?
options: Its blue light tricks the brain into thinking it is daytime.
It has no effect at all.

questions.prompt: Why is the content of phones also a problem?
options: Exciting or worrying content keeps the mind active when it should wind down.
It is too quiet to notice.

questions.prompt: What do scientists suggest?
options: Keeping phones out of the bedroom or away an hour before sleep.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 睡前在床上玩手机)、细节(B 蓝光让大脑误以为是白天)、细节(B 刺激/担忧内容让大脑亢奋)、主旨/推断(B 睡前把手机收起来)。

sets.passage: The old stone bridge had connected the two halves of the town for over two hundred years. Generations had crossed it to work, to trade, and to meet. It was more than a way over the river; it was the very heart of the community, a place where people stopped to talk and children played. Then, one terrible spring, a great flood swept it away in a single night. The loss was felt deeply. Without the bridge, a short walk became a long journey around the river. Worse, the two sides of the town, once joined, began to drift apart. Old friends met less often; the shared life of the town slowly weakened. People realised that the bridge had given them something they had never noticed until it was gone: a reason to come together. Rebuilding it was not easy. The town was not rich, and the cost was high. But the people were determined. They raised money, gave their labour, and worked together for two years. When the new bridge was finally finished, the whole town gathered to celebrate, and the old life began to return. The story of the bridge holds a quiet lesson. The things that connect us — whether bridges, traditions, or simple shared spaces — are often invisible until they are lost. A community is held together not only by the people in it, but by the countless small connections between them, connections worth protecting before, not after, they are gone.

questions.prompt: What was the old bridge to the town?
options: The heart of the community, more than a way over the river.

questions.prompt: What happened to the town when the bridge was lost?
options: The two sides of the town began to drift apart.

questions.prompt: How was the bridge rebuilt?
options: The townspeople raised money and worked together for two years.
A distant government paid for everything.

questions.prompt: What is the quiet lesson of the story?
options: That the things that connect us are often invisible until lost, and worth protecting.
That towns should not be built near rivers.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 不只是过河之路、更是社区核心)、细节(B 两岸渐渐疏远)、细节(B 镇民筹钱出力、共建两年)、主旨(B 连接我们的东西常在失去后才被察觉、值得提前守护)。

sets.passage: In countless small towns and villages, the people who rush towards danger when fire breaks out are not full-time professionals, but ordinary citizens: volunteer firefighters. By day, they are teachers, farmers, shopkeepers and mechanics. But when the alarm sounds, they drop everything and answer the call, risking their lives to protect their neighbours, and asking for nothing in return. Becoming a volunteer firefighter is no small commitment. It requires long hours of training, learning to handle equipment, fight fires, and rescue people safely. Volunteers must be ready to leave their work, their meals, or their beds at any hour of the day or night. The work is dangerous, exhausting, and entirely unpaid. Why, then, do they do it? When asked, most give a simple answer: because their community needs them. In places too small to afford a professional fire service, these volunteers are the only protection against disaster. Many also speak of the deep bond they share, the pride of serving, and the knowledge that they may, one day, save a life. The volunteer firefighter is a quiet hero of modern life, a reminder that a community depends not only on paid services, but on the willingness of ordinary people to help one another. In an age that often celebrates wealth and fame, these volunteers offer a different and older idea of what makes a life valuable: not what we take, but what we are willing to give.

questions.prompt: Who are volunteer firefighters?
options: Ordinary citizens — teachers, farmers, shopkeepers — who volunteer.

questions.prompt: What does becoming a volunteer firefighter require?
options: Long training and readiness to respond at any hour, all unpaid.

questions.prompt: Why do they do it?
What idea about a valuable life do they offer?
options: That wealth makes a life valuable.
That a valuable life is measured by what we give, not what we take.

sets.explain_zh: 细节(B 有日常职业的普通市民)、细节(B 长期训练、随时待命、全无报酬)、细节(B 因为社区需要他们)、主旨/态度(B 生命的价值在于给予而非索取)。
